agent_output: {"handoff_brief":{"recipient":"Shaq","task_context":"Implement API rate limiting middleware. Four agents have contributed: Bird defined the domain rules (100 req/60s rolling window, 429+Retry-After), MJ proposed Redis-based sliding window counters, Kobe reviewed MJ's design and identified a boundary-burst flaw recommending sorted sets instead, and Pippen specified operational requirements (fail-open, metrics, dynamic Retry-After). This brief synthesizes all four into a single implementation spec.","domain_rules":[{"rule":"Rate limit is 100 requests per 60-second rolling window per API key","testable_assertion":"Any API key that has made fewer than 100 requests in the trailing 60 seconds receives a successful response"},{"rule":"Exceeding the limit returns HTTP 429 with a Retry-After header","testable_assertion":"The 101st request within a 60-second rolling window returns status 429 and includes a Retry-After header whose value is the number of seconds until the oldest request in the window expires"},{"rule":"The window is truly rolling (sliding), not fixed","testable_assertion":"A burst of 100 requests at second 59 of one fixed window does NOT allow another 100 requests at second 0 of the next fixed window — the rolling window correctly counts all requests in the trailing 60 seconds"},{"rule":"Rate limiter fails open when Redis is unavailable","testable_assertion":"When Redis is unreachable, requests are allowed through (not rejected) and the redis_unavailable_total metric increments"}],"architecture_guidance":[{"decision":"Use Redis sorted sets for a true sliding window (not fixed-window counters)","implementation_note":"MJ originally proposed key pattern rate_limit:{api_key}:{window_start_unix} with simple counters. Kobe identified this as a fixed window vulnerable to boundary bursts. Use a single sorted set per API key (key: rate_limit:{api_key}). Each request adds a member with score = current Unix timestamp in milliseconds. On each request: (1) ZREMRANGEBYSCORE to remove entries older than now - 60s, (2) ZCARD to count remaining entries, (3) if count < 100, ZADD the new request. Wrap in a Redis pipeline or Lua script for atomicity."},{"decision":"Set TTL on sorted set keys for automatic cleanup","implementation_note":"Set EXPIRE of 61 seconds on the sorted set key after each write. This ensures keys for inactive API keys are garbage collected without a separate cleanup job."},{"decision":"Middleware runs before all authenticated routes","implementation_note":"Register the rate limiting middleware in the pipeline after authentication (so api_key is available) but before any route handler execution."},{"decision":"Retry-After header is dynamically computed from the oldest entry in the window","implementation_note":"Pippen specified Retry-After must not be a fixed value. With sorted sets: use ZRANGE to get the oldest entry's timestamp, compute Retry-After = (oldest_timestamp + 60s) - now. This is the number of seconds until one slot frees up."},{"decision":"Fail-open on Redis failure","implementation_note":"Wrap all Redis calls in try/catch. On any Redis exception (connection timeout, unavailability), allow the request through, log a warning, and increment redis_unavailable_total counter. Do NOT reject requests when the rate limiter is degraded."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"criterion":"101st request in rolling window is rejected","given":"An API key has made exactly 100 requests within the last 60 seconds","when":"A 101st request arrives","then":"HTTP 429 is returned with a Retry-After header containing the seconds until the oldest request in the window ages out"},{"criterion":"Window resets after oldest request expires","given":"An API key hit the 100-request limit and the oldest request in the window was 55 seconds ago","when":"5 seconds pass and a new request arrives","then":"The request succeeds with HTTP 200 because the oldest request has left the 60-second window"},{"criterion":"No boundary burst vulnerability","given":"An API key sends 99 requests at T=59s and 1 request at T=60s (spanning a hypothetical fixed-window boundary)","when":"The key sends another request at T=61s","then":"Only 1 slot is available (the T=0 slots have not expired yet), correctly enforcing the rolling window"},{"criterion":"Fail-open when Redis is down","given":"Redis is unavailable","when":"Any request arrives","then":"The request is allowed through, redis_unavailable_total metric is incremented, and a warning is logged"},{"criterion":"Metrics are emitted","given":"Normal operation","when":"A request is rate-limited OR Redis is unavailable","then":"rate_limit_exceeded_total or redis_unavailable_total counters are incremented respectively"}],"terminology_alignment":[{"domain_term":"Rolling window","technical_term":"Sliding window via Redis sorted set","definition":"A continuously moving 60-second time window that counts all requests from (now - 60s) to now, as opposed to a fixed window that resets at discrete boundaries"},{"domain_term":"API key","technical_term":"Authenticated principal identifier","definition":"The key extracted from the authentication middleware that uniquely identifies the caller for rate limiting purposes"},{"domain_term":"Retry-After","technical_term":"TTL of oldest sorted set member relative to window expiry","definition":"Number of seconds the client should wait before retrying; dynamically computed as (oldest_request_timestamp + 60) - current_time"}],"contradictions_resolved":[{"conflict":"MJ proposed a sliding window counter with key pattern rate_limit:{api_key}:{window_start_unix} (effectively a fixed window). Kobe identified this allows boundary bursts where a client could fire 200 requests across a window boundary, violating Bird's rolling window domain rule.","resolution":"Adopted Kobe's recommendation: use Redis sorted sets with one key per API key. Each request is a scored member (score=timestamp). ZREMRANGEBYSCORE prunes stale entries. This implements a true rolling window satisfying Bird's domain requirement."},{"conflict":"Pippen specified Retry-After as TTL of the current window key, which assumed MJ's fixed-window key pattern. With sorted sets there is no single window key TTL.","resolution":"Retry-After is now computed from the oldest member in the sorted set: (oldest_score + 60s) - now. This gives the exact number of seconds until one request slot frees up, which is more accurate than a fixed-window TTL."}],"open_questions":[]},"escalations":[],"confidence":{"level":92,"high_confidence_areas":["Domain rules are clear and unambiguous from Bird","Sorted set approach resolves the boundary-burst flaw identified by Kobe","Fail-open and metrics requirements from Pippen are straightforward","Retry-After computation logic is well-defined"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether a Lua script or MULTI/EXEC pipeline is preferred for atomicity — Shaq should decide based on Redis version and team conventions","Exact metric label names and whether a histogram for latency is also desired"],"assumptions":["Redis is already available in the infrastructure (Pippen's concern about availability implies it exists but may have downtime)","Authentication middleware already extracts and exposes the API key before this middleware runs","The 100 requests per 60 seconds limit is uniform across all API keys (no tiered limits)"]}}
agent_output_excerpt: {"handoff_brief":{"recipient":"Shaq","task_context":"Implement API rate limiting middleware. Four agents have contributed: Bird defined the domain rules (100 req/60s rolling window, 429+Retry-After), MJ proposed Redis-based sliding window counters, Kobe reviewed MJ's design and identified a boundary-burst flaw recommending sorted sets instead, and Pippen specified operational requirements (fail-open, metrics, dynamic Retry-After). This brief synthesizes all four into a single implementation spec

content.thinking:
Let me analyze the four agent outputs for consistency before producing the handoff brief.

Bird: 100 req/60s rolling window, 429 + Retry-After
MJ: Sliding window counter in Redis, key pattern with window_start_unix, TTL 60s
Kobe: Flags that MJ's window_start_unix is a fixed window, not truly sliding — recommends sorted sets
Pippen: Fail open, metrics, Retry-After = TTL of current window key

There's a contradiction between MJ and Kobe: MJ says "sliding window counter" but Kobe points out the key pattern is actually a fixed window that allows boundary bursts. Kobe recommends sorted sets for a true sliding window.
 
So Kobe is actually reviewing MJ's design and identifying a flaw—the implementation uses fixed windows despite the sliding window label. The real tension is architectural: MJ's approach with fixed-window-style counters versus Kobe's recommendation for sorted sets. And if we adopt Kobe's sorted set approach, Pippen's Retry-After calculation tied to the current window key's TTL would need rethinking. So the fix is straightforward — switch to sorted sets for the true sliding window behavior, and adjust Pippen's Retry-After logic accordingly. This isn't a fundamental contradiction, just Kobe catching an implementation flaw that I can resolve in the synthesis. Now I'll put together the handoff brief.

current_time"}],"contradictions_resolved":[{"conflict":"MJ proposed a sliding window counter with key pattern rate_limit:{api_key}:{window_start_unix} (effectively a fixed window). Kobe identified this allows boundary bursts where a client could fire 200 requests across a window boundary, violating Bird's rolling window domain rule.","resolution":"Adopted Kobe's recommendation: use Redis sorted sets with one key per API key. This gives the exact number of seconds until one request slot frees up, which is more accurate than a fixed-window TTL."}],"open_questions":[]},"escalations":[],"confidence":{"level":92,"high_confidence_areas":["Domain rules are clear and unambiguous from Bird","Sorted set approach resolves the boundary-burst flaw identified by Kobe","Fail-open and metrics requirements from Pippen are straightforward","Retry-After computation logic is well-defined"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether a Lua script or MULTI/EXEC pipeline is preferred for atomicity — Shaq should decide based on Redis version and team conventions","Exact metric label names and whether a histogram for latency is also desired"],"assumptions":["Redis is already available in the infrastructure (Pippen's concern about availability implies it exists but may have downtime)","Authentication middleware already extracts and exposes the API key before this middleware runs","The 100 requests per 60 seconds limit is uniform across all API keys (no tiered limits)"]}}
